keynote.title: Strengthening Africa’s Financial Agency in a Multipolar World

speaker.name: Professor Kevin Chika Urama
speaker.title: Chief Economist and Vice President for Economic Governance and Knowledge Management

keynote.plenary_session: Strengthening Africa’s Financial Agency in a Multipolar World

summary.executive_summary: Professor Kevin Chika Urama argued that the emergence of a multipolar global order gives Africa greater choice, bargaining power and opportunities for regional integration, but only if the continent strengthens its financial agency and acts collectively. Africa is recording relatively strong and broad-based economic growth, yet this growth remains insufficiently inclusive, creates too few jobs and has not produced adequate structural transformation. At the same time, the continent faces enormous development financing gaps, weak tax productivity, shallow financial markets, rising debt-service burdens and substantial losses through corruption, illicit financial flows, profit shifting, inefficient public investment and unfavorable risk perceptions. Urama emphasized that Africa is not fundamentally short of resources. Significant capital is available through improved tax and non-tax revenue collection, diaspora resources, pension funds, insurance assets, sovereign wealth funds, banks and other institutional investors. The central challenge is converting these resources into productive investments through stronger institutions, transparent public financial management, effective regulation, digitalization, regional coordination and better public services. He called for implementation of existing African mechanisms, including the New African Financing Architecture for Development, the African Financial Stability Mechanism, the African Monetary Institute and the African Continental Free Trade Area. Africa, he concluded, must help shape the emerging global financial architecture rather than remain subject to rules created by others.
summary.purpose: To assess Africa’s position in an emerging multipolar world, examine its development financing and fiscal constraints, identify underused domestic resources, and propose pathways for strengthening the continent’s financial sovereignty.
summary.central_argument: Africa can become an active shaper of the emerging global financial order by mobilizing its own resources, improving fiscal and economic governance, integrating its markets and implementing the financial institutions and mechanisms it has already created.

main_themes.description: A more diverse global system offers Africa additional partners, technologies, markets and bargaining options, but the continent must negotiate collectively rather than as fragmented national economies.
Africa is achieving strong and broad-based growth, but its resources are not generating adequate value addition, employment, inclusion or poverty reduction.
The continent faces major financing gaps while tax productivity, non-tax revenue, financial depth and access to long-term capital remain inadequate.
Africa possesses extensive natural and financial resources, but loses capital through inefficient spending, corruption, illicit flows, profit shifting and poor risk pricing.
Transparency, digital public-finance systems, predictable regulation and better service delivery are necessary to build trust and mobilize domestic capital.
Existing continental institutions and financing mechanisms must be implemented and coordinated to reduce fragmentation, manage risks and mobilize investment at scale.

key_messages.message: Africa must move from complaining about global financial rules to actively shaping them.
Multipolarity can increase Africa’s choices and bargaining power if countries act together.
Strong growth is insufficient unless it creates jobs, reduces poverty and accelerates structural transformation.
Africa’s financing challenge is partly a resource-management problem rather than simply a shortage of money.
Improving revenue mobilization without addressing public-investment inefficiency will not produce financial sovereignty.
Diaspora resources and African institutional assets can be converted into productive development finance.
Africa must implement its own financial architecture before the emerging global system is designed without it.

challenges_and_opportunities.text: African countries face high and often unjustified risk premiums and the widest average credit spreads globally.
Growth remains insufficiently inclusive, job creation is weak and structural transformation is uneven.
Tax productivity, compliance and non-tax revenue mobilization remain weak despite nominal revenue growth.
Corruption, illicit financial flows, revenue leakages and inefficient public investment reduce fiscal sovereignty.
Multipolarity offers Africa more technology partners, trade options and bargaining power.
Domestic revenue improvements, diaspora resources and institutional assets provide major untapped sources of development finance.

evidence_and_examples.text: Thirty-six African countries were projected to improve their growth performance, with 22 growing above 5 percent and six above 7 percent.
The estimated financing gap for structural transformation was stated as approximately $402 billion.
The speaker cited potential additional mobilization of about $469.4 billion in tax revenue and $311.6 billion in non-tax revenue without increasing tax rates.
African pension funds, banks, insurance companies and related institutions were described as holding approximately $4 trillion in assets under management.
Weaknesses in Africa’s debt architecture were said to cost the continent roughly $74 billion annually.

policy_implications: Financial sovereignty requires stronger domestic revenue systems and more efficient use of public resources.
Regional integration can increase Africa’s market depth, bargaining power and ability to manage external shocks.
Macroeconomic stability and credible institutions are essential for mobilizing long-term domestic and international capital.
Better public services and accountability can strengthen the social contract and improve voluntary tax compliance.
Africa must participate actively in reforming the global financial architecture and the pricing of African risk.
recommendations: Prioritize macroeconomic stability, risk mitigation and regional integration.
Broaden tax bases, simplify tax administration and strengthen non-tax revenue collection without relying only on higher tax rates.
Reduce corruption, illicit financial flows, revenue leakages and inefficient public expenditure.
Improve public-investment efficiency and connect taxation more visibly to quality public services.
Use digital systems to increase transparency, accountability and administrative efficiency.
Create investment instruments that enable pension funds, insurers, banks, diaspora communities, youth and women to participate in development finance.
commitments_and_announcements: The African Development Bank is supporting work on the New African Financing Architecture for Development.
The speaker referenced his involvement in developing the African Financial Stability Mechanism with the African Union Commission.
calls_to_action: African countries should speak and negotiate with a stronger collective voice.
Governments and institutions should implement existing continental financial mechanisms rather than continue creating unused frameworks.
Africa should mobilize domestic and diaspora capital for productive investment.
Policymakers should ensure that the continent helps design the emerging global financial architecture.
African institutions should create mechanisms that place youth and women at the center of economic policy and development.
partnerships_and_stakeholders: New African Financing Architecture for Development
summary.concluding_message: Africa must act now, collectively and confidently, to mobilize its own resources, implement its continental institutions and shape the new global financial architecture. Remaining silent or fragmented would leave the continent subject to rules and decisions made by others.

powerful_quotes.quote: You are either on the menu of global governance discussions, or you are just a complainant.
powerful_quotes.context: Explaining why economic, technological, political and fiscal power determine influence in the global system.

powerful_quotes.quote: We have a chance to unite better and have a greater bargaining voice as one Africa.
powerful_quotes.context: Describing the opportunity created by a multipolar world.

powerful_quotes.quote: The growth is not inclusive, it is jobless, and structural transformation is uneven.
powerful_quotes.context: Assessing the limitations of Africa’s recent economic growth.

powerful_quotes.quote: Africa is the treasure trove of resources for development.
powerful_quotes.context: Emphasizing the continent’s strategic natural-resource endowment.

powerful_quotes.quote: If we keep silent until the global financial architecture is created, we will remain on the menu as we have been before.
powerful_quotes.context: Warning that Africa must participate in shaping the emerging international financial order.

highlights.text: Africa must shape, rather than merely react to, the emerging multipolar financial order.
Multipolarity creates more choices and bargaining power, especially when Africa acts collectively.
Africa’s growth remains job-poor, insufficiently inclusive and weakly connected to structural transformation.
Hundreds of billions of dollars could be mobilized through stronger tax and non-tax revenue systems without increasing tax rates.
Domestic institutional assets and diaspora capital should be converted into productive development finance.
Existing African financial mechanisms must be implemented urgently and coordinated at continental level.

uncertain_items: The spoken title is transcribed inconsistently as 'Trending Africa’s Financial Agency in a Multiple World'; the intended title appears to be 'Strengthening Africa’s Financial Agency in a Multipolar World.'
Some institutional names and acronyms in the transcript are distorted by automatic transcription.
The precise event date and location were not clearly stated in the keynote transcript.
A small number of statistics and their exact phrasing may require verification against the speaker’s presentation slides.
